boundary: This audit maps EVP-8-HARD paper claims to tracked aggregate evidence. It does not call model APIs, read raw model responses, read patch diffs, modify prompts, or infer new model outputs.

forbidden_claims.claim: The EVP-8-HARD system is a reliable automatic patch correctness verifier.
forbidden_claims.reason: Tool-contestation does not strictly reject the nine known tool false accepts.

forbidden_claims.claim: Qwen or DeepSeek outperforms the deterministic tool-only baseline as an automatic merge gate.
forbidden_claims.reason: With verdict, both models match the tool baseline; without verdict, gains are mostly escalation and correct recall collapses under tool-contestation.

forbidden_claims.claim: The LLM semantically identifies the wrong patches in the opportunity set.
forbidden_claims.reason: Strict reject is 0/9 for both tool-contestation models.

forbidden_claims.claim: The 47-candidate controlled cohort proves performance on real agent patch distributions.
forbidden_claims.reason: The cohort is controlled and small; realistic hard-negative acquisition remains a separate boundary.

forbidden_claims.claim: More visible evidence monotonically improves verifier quality.
forbidden_claims.reason: E6 with verdict repeats tool false accepts; tool-contestation improves safety by escalation but loses recall.

one_sentence_argument: In controlled candidate patch verification, EVP-8-HARD shows that verdict-like tool evidence can dominate LLM decisions, while explicit tool-contestation can reduce unsafe autonomous accepts through escalation, but the evidence supports risk triage rather than autonomous correctness verification.

paper_section_scaffold.job: Show that Qwen and DeepSeek match tool-only under with-verdict.
Show decision shifts after removing verdict fields and adding contestation.
Show when conservative escalation is useful and when automation recall is lost.
Interpret the result as risk triage, not autonomous verification.

qualified_claims.claim: The system can be positioned as an evidence-aware risk controller, not as an autonomous merge gate.
qualified_claims.condition: Only valid when escalation is treated as human-review routing and not as automatic correctness verification.

qualified_claims.claim: The observed LLM-added value is policy behavior over evidence, not semantic proof.
qualified_claims.condition: Supported because known false accepts become escalations, while strict reject remains zero on the primary opportunity set.

statistical_boundary: Intervals are wide because the hard-case cohort has 47 candidates and the primary opportunity set has only nine known false accepts. Use these results as evidence of behavior shift and triage tradeoff, not as a reliability guarantee.

supported_claims.allowed_wording: We study a controlled 47-candidate hard-case cohort with hidden evaluator labels.
supported_claims.claim: EVP-8-HARD is a controlled 47-candidate hard-case cohort with 10 correct and 37 incorrect candidates.
supported_claims.evidence_summary: All audited systems report 47 records, 10 correct candidates, and 37 incorrect candidates.

supported_claims.allowed_wording: With a visible verdict-like tool summary, both models reproduced the deterministic baseline.
supported_claims.claim: When the verdict-like deterministic merge-gate summary is visible, Qwen and DeepSeek reproduce the tool-only baseline decisions on this cohort.
supported_claims.evidence_summary: Tool-only, Qwen with-verdict, and DeepSeek with-verdict all have accept=17, reject=30, false_accept=9, false_reject=2, and no escalation.

supported_claims.allowed_wording: Removing verdict-like fields changed decisions but did not produce strict corrections of known false accepts.
supported_claims.claim: Removing verdict-like fields partially decouples LLM decisions from the tool baseline, but does not yield strict correction of known false accepts.
supported_claims.evidence_summary: On the nine tool false accepts, Qwen evidence-only repeats seven accepts and DeepSeek evidence-only repeats four; strict reject remains 0.

supported_claims.allowed_wording: Tool-contestation shifted known false accepts primarily from accept to escalation.
supported_claims.claim: Explicit tool-contestation reduces unsafe autonomous accepts mainly by routing known tool false accepts to escalation.
supported_claims.evidence_summary: On the nine tool false accepts, Qwen tool-contestation escalates eight and repeats one accept; DeepSeek escalates all nine. Strict reject is 0/9.

supported_claims.allowed_wording: Under policies where false accepts are costly, contestation can be useful as a conservative triage layer.
supported_claims.claim: Tool-contestation is useful under policies where false accepts are much more expensive than human escalation, but this utility is conditional.
supported_claims.evidence_summary: DeepSeek tool-contestation wins 16/20 sensitivity cells, but correct recall is 0 because all correct accepted-by-tool patches are escalated or rejected.

terminology_ledger.definition: The 47-candidate controlled hard-case cohort used in this branch.
terminology_ledger.usage: Use as cohort name, not as a general benchmark claim.

terminology_ledger.definition: The E6 setting where a verdict-like deterministic merge-gate summary is visible.
The ablation removing verdict-like fields while preserving lower-level visible evidence.
The prompt variant asking the model to challenge visible-test-only accept premises.
Rejecting a known tool false accept.
Rejecting or escalating a known tool false accept.
